content.full_text:
March 29, 1979

I. E. Bonde
113 Princeton Road
Menlo Park CA 94025

Re: 7903-06
Bonding Requirements under
the Political Reform Act
of 1974

Dear Mr. Bonde:

In response to your letter of March 24th, enclosed is a copy
of the Political Reform Act of 1974. The provision you were
concerned with appears at Section 91012 (page 62). You will
note that the bonding requirement applies where a private
complainant has filed a civil action pursuant to Section 91007
(page 61).

Please feel free to contact us if you need any further
information.

Very truly yours,


Robert D. Blasier, Jr.
Staff or
Enforcement Division

RDB:pvp
Enclosure

...nal Revenue launch probes

Investigation of Rhodes widened

By Jim Wilson
Staff Writer

FREMONT — The Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Internal Revenue Service have opened investigations of Fremont Mayor Gene Rhodes, the Mercury learned Thursday.

Sources close to both investigations said the agencies were looking into Rhodes'complicated financial dealings to see if any federal laws had been violated.

The Mercury has also learned that he U.S. Attorney's Office is reviewing he Rhodes case. It has been assigned to senior assistant U.S. Attorney Richard [illegible], sources said.

Spokesmen at the U.S. Attorney's Office in San Francisco, as well as FBI and IRS offices there, refused comment.

All three agencies said they are barred by law from commenting on investigations. All said they could neither confirm nor deny that a probe was under way or even contemplated.

Other sources have confirmed, however, that both IRS and FBI investigators have been seeking information on Rhodes from local officials and private citizens.

Rhodes currently is involved in lawsuits filed by the Alameda County District Attorney's Office and private attorneys and is the subject of a recall movement by a group of Fremont residents.

The two lawsuits were filed after the Mercury reported a year ago that Rhodes failed to disclose all financial interests required by state law and voted on matters before the council involving economic holdings.

Rhodes, while refusing to comment on any of the charges against him, has consistently denied any guilt.

Meanwhile, the private lawsuit against Rhodes has run into what could be a major setback.

The state Court of Appeal has ruled that two Fremont residents represented by San Jose attorney Lee Sanders must post a cash bond to continue the case.

The Alameda County Superior Court now must decide what amount the plaintiffs, Juanita Anderson and Emmett Nannery, must post, basing the decision on its assessment of the merits of the case.

The bond requirement, included in the state Political Reform Act of 1974, is designed to discourage frivolous lawsuits aimed at harassing public offi-

29142
3.27.99

STATE OF CALIF
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE COMMISSION
P.O. BOX 207
SACRAMENTO CALIF 95814

GENTLEMEN:
I NOTICE ON THE ENCLOSED PAPER
ON JIMMY RHODES THAT SOME KIND OF
BOND REQUIREMENT IS PART OF THE 1974
POLITICAL REFORM ACT.
I WOULD LIKE A COPY OF THE
REQUIREMENT AND DATE IT WENT INTO
EFFECT. IN 1974 I WON HARASHA
THROUGH THE COURT SYSTEM BY A
RETAIR ATTORNEY. HIS COST WERE NOT
TO NORMINE, BUT WHILE UNTO THE
THOUSAND OF DOLLARS.
& NOW I AM LOST OUNDERING IF
I DID MUST A GOOD POINT.

Sincerely

J E BRAY
113 PRINCETON
INCHLO PARK CA
94025

embedding.qa_text: March 29, 1979 I. E. Bonde 113 Princeton Road Menlo Park CA 94025 Re: 7903-06 Bonding Requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974 Dear Mr. Bonde: In response to your letter of March 24th, enclosed is a copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974. The provision you were concerned with appears at Section 91012 (page 62). You will note that the bonding requirement applies where a private complainant has filed a civil action pursuant to Section 91007 (page 61). Please feel free to contact us if you need any further information. Very truly yours, Robert D. Blasier, Jr. Stafftor Enforcement Division RDB:pvp Enclosure The article titled "Investigation of Rhodes widened" by Jim Wilson, Staff Writer, reports on the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Internal Revenue Service opening investigations into Fremont Mayor Gene Rhodes. Sources close to both investigations said the agencies were looking into Rhodes' complicated financial dealings to see if any federal laws had been violated. Spokesmen at the U.S. Attorney's Office in San Francisco, as well as FBI and IRS offices there, refused comment. All three agencies said they are barred by law from commenting on investigations. All said that they could neither confirm nor deny that a probe was under way or even contemplated. Other sources have confirmed, however, that both IRS and FBI investigators have been seeking information on Rhodes from local officials and private citizens. Rhodes currently is involved in lawsuits filed by the Alameda County District Attorney's Office and private attorneys and is the subject of a recall movement by a group of Fremont residents. The two lawsuits were filed after the Mercury reported a year ago that Rhodes failed to disclose all his financial interests required by state law and voted on matters before the council involving economic holdings. Rhodes, while refusing to comment on any of the charges against him, has consistently denied any guilt. Meanwhile, the private lawsuit against Rhodes has run into what could be a major setback. The state Court of Appeal has ruled that two Fremont residents represented by San Jose attorney Lee Sanders must post a cash bond to continue the case. The Alameda County Superior Court now must decide what amount the plaintiffs, Juanita Anderson and Emmett Nannery, must post, basing the decision on its assessment of the merits of the case. The bond requirement, included in the state Political Reform Act of 1974, is designed to discourage frivolous lawsuits aimed at harassing public officials. STATE OF CALIF. FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE COMMISSION P.O. BOX 807 SACRAMENTO, CALIF. 95841 Gentlemen: I noticed on the enclosed article by Norm Rhodes that some kind of bond requirement is part of the 1974 Political Reform Act. I would like a copy of this requirement and date it went into effect. In 1974 I was harassed through the court system by a Rhodes attorney. All costs were next to nothing, but never less than the thousands of dollars. So now I am not wondering if I did miss
